article:
**Assumptions used for this report:** I use **HGT** in the strict sense of **non-sexual transfer of genetic information across normal mating barriers**, not ordinary introgression/hybridization; I treat **organelle-to-nucleus endosymbiotic gene transfer** as important background but not the main benchmark for disputed plant/animal HGT claims; and I include **plant mitochondrial transfers** and **endogenous viral integrations** where the recent literature treats them as major parts of the evidence base for non-microbial eukaryotes. The emphasis is on **plants and animals**, with microbes used mainly as the comparative baseline. [[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7589212/)[[2]](http://www.ask-force.org/web/HorizontalGT/Keeling-HGT-Eukaryotic-Evolution-2008.pdf)[[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3429517/)[[36]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10377300/)

# Horizontal Gene Transfer in Non-Microbial Eukaryotes: Plants, Animals, Evidence, Mechanisms, Rarity, and Evolutionary Significance

## Introduction and scope definition

**Horizontal gene transfer (HGT)** is the movement of genetic information between lineages outside parent-offspring inheritance and outside sexual reproduction. In non-microbial eukaryotes, the key question is not simply whether DNA can enter a cell, but whether foreign sequence can enter a plant or animal lineage, become integrated into a heritable genome, persist through vertical transmission, and in some cases acquire function in a highly constrained regulatory environment. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7589212/)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)

That distinction matters because eukaryotic discussions often blur several biologically different processes. **Introgression** moves genes through hybridization and backcrossing and is therefore not HGT in the strict sense used here. **Endosymbiotic gene transfer (EGT)** from mitochondria and plastids is a genuine transfer process, but it is tied to organelle evolution and is not the right benchmark for controversial claims that free-living bacteria, fungi, plants, or animals have repeatedly donated functional genes into plant and animal nuclear genomes. Reviews explicitly treat these as related but separable categories. [[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7589212/)[[2]](http://www.ask-force.org/web/HorizontalGT/Keeling-HGT-Eukaryotic-Evolution-2008.pdf)[[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3429517/)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)

The current consensus is a middle position that is stronger than the old skepticism but much narrower than the most expansive genomic-era claims. It is **no longer credible to say HGT never matters in plants and animals**, because multiple well-supported cases now exist in both groups. But it is also **not credible to describe plant and animal nuclear genomes as HGT-dominated in the microbial sense**, because most validated studies still find that only a small minority of genes in most eukaryotic nuclear genomes are detectably foreign in origin—typically **at most a few percent**, and often less. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)

The most analytically useful framing is therefore comparative. In microbes, HGT is a routine engine of adaptation and genome turnover. In plants and animals, HGT is better understood as a **rare, strongly filtered source of novelty** that succeeds only when ecological exposure, DNA transfer, chromosomal integration, expression compatibility, and population-level fixation all align. That makes eukaryotic HGT less important in bulk, but often more interesting case by case: the successful transfers are disproportionately those that solve a specific ecological problem such as detoxification, plant cell-wall digestion, defense, parasitism, or host interaction. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4962295/)

### Historical trajectory of the field

A short timeline clarifies why the subject remains both accepted and contentious:

- **Early genomic era:** broad claims of foreign genes in animals, including humans, were advanced and then largely rejected after better taxon sampling and phylogenetic reanalysis. [[13]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11418856/)[[17]](https://www.jcvi.org/media-center/more-extensive-analysis-casts-doubt-claim-bacterial-genes-humans)[[15]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13059-017-1214-2)
- **2008:** Keeling and Palmer’s review helped set the modern evidentiary baseline: phylogenetic conflict is central, but contamination, hidden paralogy, and alternative histories must be excluded before claiming HGT in eukaryotes. [[2]](http://www.ask-force.org/web/HorizontalGT/Keeling-HGT-Eukaryotic-Evolution-2008.pdf)
- **2010s:** several strong adaptive animal cases emerged, including carotenoid biosynthesis in aphids and plant cell-wall-degrading enzymes in nematodes, while experimentally tractable mechanisms such as graft-mediated chloroplast transfer in plants and Wolbachia-to-host insertions in animals clarified plausible routes. [[64]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/)[[51]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/)[[84]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3289295/)[[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)
- **2015–2017:** the tardigrade “17.5% foreign genes” episode became a defining cautionary case after contamination-aware reassembly reduced the estimate to **at most 1–2%**, with only about **0.2%** strong bacterial and **0.2%** strong non-metazoan-eukaryote candidates. [[21]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1510461112)[[24]](https://www.pacb.com/publications/no-evidence-for-extensive-horizontal-gene-transfer-in-the-genome-of-the-tardigrade-hypsibius-dujardini/)[[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)
- **2024–2026:** recent syntheses accept bona fide eukaryotic HGT as real but emphasize that most nuclear genomes contain only **a few percent at most** detectably foreign genes, while plant-specific reanalyses argue that many published interkingdom cases collapse under improved taxon sampling and “last-one-out” differential-loss models. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[29]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1789570/full)

## Background: how HGT is established and studied in microbes versus plants and animals

In bacteria and archaea, HGT is anchored by well-characterized mechanisms—**transformation**, **conjugation**, and **transduction**—and by population-genetic conditions that make horizontally acquired genes both common and rapidly selectable. Some syntheses estimate that roughly **75–80%** of genes in a prokaryotic genome have experienced HGT at some point in their history, although the precise proportion depends on method and evolutionary model. [[3]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4536854/)[[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/veterinary-science-and-veterinary-medicine/horizontal-gene-transfer)[[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4962295/)

That microbial baseline is useful but also misleading if imported uncritically into plant or animal genomics. In microbes, the cell that acquires DNA is often itself the reproductive unit; there is no separate germline to cross, and transferred genes can immediately alter metabolism, resistance, or niche use. In multicellular plants and animals, the same sequence must usually survive a much longer chain of filters: entry into a relevant cell, nuclear access, chromosomal integration, escape from silencing or deletion, compatibility with eukaryotic transcription and splicing, transmission through reproductive tissues, and eventual fixation in a population. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)[[77]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4033532/)

A second difference is interpretive. In microbes, a patchy gene distribution or a strongly discordant gene tree is often evaluated against a prior expectation that HGT is frequent. In plants and animals, the prior is lower, so the same pattern is less probative. A gene found in fungi and one plant lineage could reflect HGT, but it could also reflect sparse sampling, hidden paralogy, or ancestral presence followed by widespread loss. The evidentiary bar must therefore be higher in non-microbial eukaryotes than in microbial systems. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[2]](http://www.ask-force.org/web/HorizontalGT/Keeling-HGT-Eukaryotic-Evolution-2008.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)

A third difference is that **exposure to foreign DNA is not necessarily the limiting variable in eukaryotes**. The 2024 *Nature Reviews Genetics* synthesis argues that earlier discussion may have overemphasized phagocytosis or endosymbiosis as routes of acquisition and underemphasized whether selection actually favors retention. In many eukaryotes, selection acts on kinds of variation not readily supplied by HGT, and foreign genes often fail because they do not integrate well into host regulatory networks. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)

### Microbes versus non-microbial eukaryotes: baseline comparison

| Dimension | Microbial systems | Plants | Animals |
|---|---|---|---|
| Canonical transfer routes | Transformation, conjugation, transduction are common and well characterized. [[78]](https://shapiro.bsd.uchicago.edu/Modes_of_Horizontal_DNA_Transfer.html) | No equally general canonical route, but **Agrobacterium-like T-DNA transfer** is directly demonstrated; grafts and parasite interfaces create repeated opportunities. [[80]](https://journals.plos.org/plospathogens/article)[[81]](https://www.annualreviews.org/doi/10.1146/annurev-phyto-082718-100101)[[82]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26968003/)[[84]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3289295/)[[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6023873/) | No canonical general route; strongest repeated route is **endosymbiont DNA leakage**, especially from Wolbachia. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[89]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3798261/) |
| Cell that acquires DNA vs heritability | Often the same cell lineage; acquisition can be inherited immediately. [[78]](https://shapiro.bsd.uchicago.edu/Modes_of_Horizontal_DNA_Transfer.html)[[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4962295/) | Heritability barrier exists but is less segregated than in animals. [[2]](http://www.ask-force.org/web/HorizontalGT/Keeling-HGT-Eukaryotic-Evolution-2008.pdf)[[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)[[105]](https://www.nature.com/articles/ncomms2148) | Strong germline barrier; somatic acquisition is usually evolutionarily irrelevant. [[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[77]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4033532/)[[79]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5791352/) |
| Typical genome-wide impact | Often very large; some syntheses estimate **75–80%** of genes have experienced HGT. [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/veterinary-science-and-veterinary-medicine/horizontal-gene-transfer)[[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4962295/) | Usually much lower; validated cases exist but most broad candidate lists are inflated. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/) | Usually much lower; strongest cases are concentrated in invertebrate hotspots, not broadly across metazoans. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/) |
| Main evidentiary difficulties | Recombination history and gene turnover, but HGT prior is high. [[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4962295/) | Contamination, taxon undersampling, differential loss, organellar confusion, viral integrations. [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[29]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1789570/full)[[36]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10377300/) | Contamination, symbiont carryover, hidden homology, sparse sampling, weak expression-only claims. [[24]](https://www.pacb.com/publications/no-evidence-for-extensive-horizontal-gene-transfer-in-the-genome-of-the-tardigrade-hypsibius-dujardini/)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2647948/) |
| Typical functional payoff of accepted cases | Resistance, metabolism, niche exploitation. [[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4962295/) | Defense, stress response, photosynthesis-related traits, parasite-host exchange, domesticated T-DNAs. [[41]](https://etheses.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/31607/1/SGSH_Thesis_Corrections_upload.pdf)[[42]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32273397/)[[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/) | Detoxification, pigmentation, plant cell-wall digestion, parasitism, occasional sex determination or defense. [[64]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/)[[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)[[51]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/)[[91]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28753988/)[[115]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-49919-1) |

One additional background distinction is important. Reviews note that organelle-related EGT was massive during early eukaryotic evolution, but that does not imply ongoing, microbial-like HGT in plants and animals. Keeling and Palmer emphasized that although organelle-to-nucleus transfer was extensive in the evolution of mitochondria and plastids, **not one case of functional EGT had been reported across roughly 600 million years of metazoan evolution** in the sense relevant to modern animal HGT debates. [[2]](http://www.ask-force.org/web/HorizontalGT/Keeling-HGT-Eukaryotic-Evolution-2008.pdf) This is another reason to separate ancient organellogenesis from contemporary claims of sporadic interkingdom gene acquisition.

## Evidence standards and methodological caveats for claiming HGT in eukaryotes

## What counts as strong evidence

Across classic and recent reviews, the **starting point** for HGT inference remains **phylogenetic incongruence**: a gene tree should strongly conflict with the accepted species tree, with sufficient taxon sampling to show that the anomalous placement is not a simple sampling artefact. Keeling and Palmer described such conflict as the “gold standard” starting point, and later reviews still treat it as the method of choice. [[2]](http://www.ask-force.org/web/HorizontalGT/Keeling-HGT-Eukaryotic-Evolution-2008.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)

In practice, however, **phylogeny alone is insufficient** for plants and animals. Strong contemporary claims typically combine several independent evidence types:

1. **Robust phylogeny** with broad donor and recipient sampling, explicit alternative-tree testing, and attention to hidden paralogy and long-branch artefacts. [[2]](http://www.ask-force.org/web/HorizontalGT/Keeling-HGT-Eukaryotic-Evolution-2008.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[29]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1789570/full)  
2. **Physical genomic integration**, ideally on chromosome-level scaffolds or validated by long reads, BACs, or PCR across host–foreign junctions. [[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)[[111]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10898089/)[[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/)[[123]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25902487/)  
3. **Contamination control**, including read-depth consistency, scaffold-level screening, exclusion of donor-rich contaminant contigs, and host-flanking sequence. [[24]](https://www.pacb.com/publications/no-evidence-for-extensive-horizontal-gene-transfer-in-the-genome-of-the-tardigrade-hypsibius-dujardini/)[[111]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10898089/)  
4. **Inheritance across individuals, populations, or related species**, which shows that the sequence is a resident component of the host lineage rather than a one-off contaminant. [[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)[[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2647948/)[[117]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9599486/)[[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/)  
5. **Accommodation to host gene architecture**, such as introns, host-like splicing, or sequence composition that suggests long-term residence and remodeling after acquisition. [[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/)[[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)[[120]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3032686/)  
6. **Expression evidence**, preferably tissue-appropriate and above trivial background. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)  
7. **Biochemical or phenotypic evidence**, such as enzyme activity, knockdown phenotypes, pathogen-resistance assays, or fitness-related effects. [[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)[[120]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3032686/)[[121]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5664137/)[[42]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32273397/)[[43]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/context/usdaarsfacpub/article/3346/viewcontent/Wang_SCIENCE_2020_Horizontal_gene_USDA.pdf)

The most reliable individual-case literature usually comes from **primary studies** that combine several of these lines at once. By contrast, large candidate inventories generated from assembly mining or best-hit methods are better treated as hypothesis-generating until they survive phylogenetic and contamination-aware reanalysis. [[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)[[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[111]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10898089/)

## Why eukaryotic HGT is hard to prove

Apparent HGT in plants and animals can be confused with at least six recurrent alternatives:

- **Contamination** during extraction, library preparation, or assembly. A 2024 *Genome Biology* contamination-screening study reported that scanning **1.6 million GenBank assemblies** identified **36.8 Gbp** of contamination, or **0.16%** of total bases, and noted contamination in **4,063 of 11,232 eukaryotic submissions** screened in 2022. This is not a minor technical nuisance; contaminated assemblies have explicitly caused false conclusions about HGT. [[111]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10898089/)
- **Assembly artefacts**, especially chimeric joins that make contaminant DNA appear physically linked to host sequence. This was central to the tardigrade controversy. [[24]](https://www.pacb.com/publications/no-evidence-for-extensive-horizontal-gene-transfer-in-the-genome-of-the-tardigrade-hypsibius-dujardini/)[[111]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10898089/)
- **Sparse taxon sampling**, which can make a gene appear bacterial-, fungal-, or plant-specific simply because homologs in other eukaryotes were not yet sequenced. This helped invalidate early human claims and is now central to plant reanalyses. [[17]](https://www.jcvi.org/media-center/more-extensive-analysis-casts-doubt-claim-bacterial-genes-humans)[[15]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13059-017-1214-2)[[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[29]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1789570/full)
- **Differential gene loss**, formalized in the recent **“last-one-out”** critique. A topology in which fungi and one plant lineage retain a gene can be explained either by HGT plus multiple losses or by ancestral presence followed by broad differential loss; the latter often requires fewer assumptions. [[29]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1789570/full)[[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)
- **Hidden paralogy** and incomplete resolution of ancient duplications, which can generate misleading placements. [[2]](http://www.ask-force.org/web/HorizontalGT/Keeling-HGT-Eukaryotic-Evolution-2008.pdf)[[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7589212/)
- **Conflation with EGT or viral endogenization**, which are real transfer processes but biologically distinct from ordinary free-living donor-to-host HGT. [[2]](http://www.ask-force.org/web/HorizontalGT/Keeling-HGT-Eukaryotic-Evolution-2008.pdf)[[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3429517/)[[36]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10377300/)

### Why “expression” is only supportive

A recurring analytical mistake in the eukaryotic HGT literature is to treat transcription as near-proof of function. Reviews explicitly reject that shortcut. Low-level transcription can reflect leaky promoters, nearby chromatin activity, or pseudogene noise. The Wolbachia insertion literature illustrates this clearly: in *Drosophila ananassae*, at least some transferred genes are transcribed, but one analysis emphasized that expression levels were estimated at roughly **10^4 to 10^7-fold lower** than the control gene *act5C*, consistent with background transcription rather than demonstrated function. [[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2647948/)[[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)

## A practical confidence rubric, executed on real cases

The original rubric can be made more useful by separating two questions that are often conflated:

- **Authenticity**: is this really an HGT-derived resident genomic sequence?
- **Adaptive significance**: is there direct evidence that it benefits the recipient?

### Worked example: applying the rubric

| Case | Phylogenetic incongruence | Host genomic integration | Contamination/inheritance control | Function evidence | Overall authenticity | Overall adaptive confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sweet potato Agrobacterium T-DNAs** | Strong, and in a donor system with a directly known transfer mechanism. [[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/)[[123]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25902487/)[[80]](https://journals.plos.org/plospathogens/article) | Supported by PCR, qPCR, Southern blotting, genome walking, and BAC sequencing. [[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/)[[123]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25902487/) | One T-DNA reported in all tested cultivated accessions and absent from close wild relatives, supporting inheritance rather than contamination. [[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/)[[123]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25902487/) | Expression shown, but direct organismal phenotype remains less sharply demonstrated than in whitefly or aphid cases. [[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/)[[123]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25902487/) | **High** | **Moderate** |
| **BtPMaT1 in whitefly** | Plant-like phylogenetic placement with broader whitefly plant-derived gene set consistent with repeated HGT. [[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)[[117]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9599486/)[[118]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36155788/) | Integrated in whitefly genomes and represented across multiple cryptic species. [[117]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9599486/)[[118]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36155788/) | Orthology across species, expression, and purifying selection argue against contamination. [[117]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9599486/)[[118]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36155788/) | Strong: plant-mediated RNAi silencing rendered tomato effectively resistant to whiteflies. [[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641) | **High** | **High** |
| **D. ananassae Wolbachia insert** | Clear donor identity. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2647948/) | Entire approximately **1.4 Mb** Wolbachia genome integrated. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/) | Integration secure; this is not a contamination dispute. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2647948/) | Weak: low-level transcription without convincing phenotype. [[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2647948/)[[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/) | **High** | **Low/uncertain** |
| **Tardigrade 17.5% foreign-gene claim** | Initial signal based on contaminated assembly. [[21]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1510461112)[[24]](https://www.pacb.com/publications/no-evidence-for-extensive-horizontal-gene-transfer-in-the-genome-of-the-tardigrade-hypsibius-dujardini/) | Collapsed after reassembly and filtering. [[24]](https://www.pacb.com/publications/no-evidence-for-extensive-horizontal-gene-transfer-in-the-genome-of-the-tardigrade-hypsibius-dujardini/) | Failed contamination-aware reanalysis. [[24]](https://www.pacb.com/publications/no-evidence-for-extensive-horizontal-gene-transfer-in-the-genome-of-the-tardigrade-hypsibius-dujardini/)[[111]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10898089/) | No robust phenotype case for the dramatic original estimate. [[24]](https://www.pacb.com/publications/no-evidence-for-extensive-horizontal-gene-transfer-in-the-genome-of-the-tardigrade-hypsibius-dujardini/) | **Artifactual/refuted** | **Artifactual/refuted** |

This worked example highlights a central conclusion of the field: **authentic transfer and adaptive domestication are different thresholds**. Some cases, such as large Wolbachia inserts, are authentic HGT-derived insertions but not compelling adaptive innovations. Others, such as **BtPMaT1**, clear both bars. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2647948/)[[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)

## Documented HGT in plants

Plant HGT is now best supported in a **small number of recurring contexts** rather than as a uniformly pervasive phenomenon across all plant genomes. The strongest evidence is concentrated in **Agrobacterium-mediated natural transgenesis**, **plant-to-plant transfer in parasitic plants and grasses**, **selected fungus-to-plant nuclear cases**, and **viral endogenization**, especially endogenous caulimovirids. At the same time, plants are also where the methodological debate is currently sharpest, because recent reanalyses have substantially narrowed many earlier interkingdom candidate lists. [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)[[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12451028/)[[29]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1789570/full)

### 1) Bacteria-to-plant HGT: the clearest natural interkingdom route

The **strongest mechanistically documented natural interkingdom HGT pathway in plants** remains **Agrobacterium-mediated DNA transfer**. Recent plant reanalyses emphasize that among natural plant interkingdom mechanisms, this is still the clearest case in which the molecular route is not only inferred but directly understood. [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[80]](https://journals.plos.org/plospathogens/article)[[81]](https://www.annualreviews.org/doi/10.1146/annurev-phyto-082718-100101)[[82]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26968003/)

The flagship case is **cultivated sweet potato**, often described as a **naturally transgenic food crop**. Two T-DNA regions, **IbT-DNA1** and **IbT-DNA2**, were identified in the sweet potato genome using multiple orthogonal methods—**PCR, quantitative PCR, Southern blotting, genome walking, and BAC sequencing**—which is exactly the kind of multi-method evidence eukaryotic HGT claims require. One T-DNA was found in all tested cultivated accessions and absent from close wild relatives, implying stable inheritance and possible fixation during domestication or domestication-related selection. [[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/)[[123]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25902487/)[[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)

Natural Agrobacterium-derived integrations are also documented in **Nicotiana glauca** and **Linaria vulgaris**, where T-DNA-like regions include genes such as **mikimopine synthase (mis)** and **rolC**. These cases are particularly valuable methodologically because they do not rely solely on patchy phylogenetic placement; they occur in a donor-recipient system with a known DNA-transfer machinery. [[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/)[[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12451028/)[[80]](https://journals.plos.org/plospathogens/article)

A related mechanistic point is that **Agrobacterium may not be uniquely capable** of this kind of transfer. The 2019 pathway review cited here notes that **Rhizobium etli CFN42** carries a complete and functional **vir** region and can mediate functional DNA transfer and stable transformation of plant cells when supplied with a T-DNA-containing vector, although low efficiency and engineered experimental conditions mean this does not yet demonstrate widespread natural HGT by rhizobia. [[80]](https://journals.plos.org/plospathogens/article)[[81]](https://www.annualreviews.org/doi/10.1146/annurev-phyto-082718-100101)[[82]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26968003/) Analytically, the importance is narrower: it shows that the Agrobacterium-like route is a real mechanistic class rather than a single idiosyncratic exception.

### 2) Plant-to-plant transfer: strongest in parasitic plants and grasses

Most classic plant-to-plant HGT cases involve **parasitic plants**, especially **mitochondrial genes** moving across haustorial host-parasite interfaces. Reviews state that most historically reported plant-to-plant HGT in flowering plants involves the mitochondria, and the first widely noted examples included transfer of the mitochondrial **atp1** gene among **Cuscuta**, **Bartsia**, and **Plantago**-related systems. [[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/)[[38]](https://www.mdpi.com/2075-1729/11/8/857)

These cases are strong for mechanistic reasons as well as phylogenetic ones. Plant mitochondria are unusually permissive to DNA uptake, and parasitic plants establish intimate vascular and cellular connections to hosts through **haustoria**. That combination lowers exposure barriers compared with ordinary donor-recipient encounters and helps explain why parasitic plant mitochondria have become a recurrent HGT hotspot. [[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/)[[38]](https://www.mdpi.com/2075-1729/11/8/857)[[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6023873/)

The strongest widely cited **nuclear** parasitic-plant case is in **Striga hermonthica**, which contains a host-like gene acquired from a grass donor related to **sorghum**. The gene clusters phylogenetically within grasses rather than with the eudicot position expected for *Striga*, appears in expressed sequence tags, and shows features consistent with recent acquisition or possible transfer through an mRNA/cDNA intermediate. [[39]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20508124/)[[40]](https://plantimmunity.riken.jp/_src/582/201020science20yoshida20et20al.pdf)[[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12451028/) This is important because it shows that plant-to-plant transfer in host-parasite systems is not restricted to mitochondria.

A second major plant-to-plant context is **grass-to-grass HGT**. In **Alloteropsis semialata**, comparative genomics identified **59 transferred genes** from at least **nine donor species**, often in foreign DNA blocks up to **170 kb**. Many genes are expressed and are associated with **photosynthesis, stress tolerance, disease resistance, senescence, and structural processes**. [[41]](https://etheses.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/31607/1/SGSH_Thesis_Corrections_upload.pdf)[[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/) This matters evolutionarily because it moves plant HGT beyond anecdotal transfers: here, multiple genes relevant to ecophysiology appear to have crossed species boundaries outside normal reproduction.

### 3) Fungus-to-plant HGT: important candidates, but now under stricter scrutiny

The best-known fungus-to-plant case is **Fhb7** in **Thinopyrum elongatum**, which encodes a **glutathione S-transferase** reported to detoxify trichothecenes and confer broad resistance to **Fusarium** pathogens. The original report found a near-identical homolog in an **Epichloë** endophyte and no homologs in the surveyed plant datasets used at the time, and it linked the gene directly to disease resistance without yield penalty when moved into wheat backgrounds. [[42]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32273397/)[[43]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/context/usdaarsfacpub/article/3346/viewcontent/Wang_SCIENCE_2020_Horizontal_gene_USDA.pdf)[[44]](https://files01.core.ac.uk/download/334984681.pdf)

If upheld, **Fhb7** is among the most striking plant cases because it combines a strong functional story with a plausible donor ecology: long-term association between grasses and fungal endophytes. But the most recent plant reanalysis literature has raised the general evidentiary bar for exactly this class of claim. The retrieved reassessment sources do not specifically overturn **Fhb7**, yet they show that many interkingdom plant HGT candidates weakened when broader homology searches uncovered additional eukaryotic homologs or when differential-loss explanations were formally considered. [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[29]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1789570/full) The correct present treatment is therefore: **high functional interest, but phylogenetic interpretation should be held to stricter current standards than early reception implied**. [[42]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32273397/)[[43]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/context/usdaarsfacpub/article/3346/viewcontent/Wang_SCIENCE_2020_Horizontal_gene_USDA.pdf)[[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[29]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1789570/full)

Another frequently cited case is a **β-1,6-glucanase** in **perennial ryegrass**, described as one of the earliest strong examples of nuclear HGT from a distantly related eukaryote into a flowering plant and potentially relevant to fungal cell-wall degradation and pathogen response. Reviews also note that several proposed grass fungal HGTs involve long-term endophytic associations such as **Epichloë**, which is mechanistically plausible because persistent intimate symbiosis increases donor exposure and the chance of retained integration. [[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12451028/)[[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)

### 4) Virus-to-plant transfer: abundant, ancient, and functionally mixed

The **most widespread foreign DNA in plant genomes may be viral rather than bacterial or fungal host genes**. Many plant genomes contain **endogenous viral elements (EVEs)**, especially **endogenous caulimovirids**, across most vascular plants. These sequences are fossils of past viral infections that became integrated and then vertically inherited. [[36]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10377300/)[[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12451028/)

This is a major part of the plant foreign-DNA landscape, but it should not be oversold as equivalent to repeated acquisition of adaptive host genes. Many EVEs are best interpreted as **infection relics** or **genomic fossils**. Their evolutionary significance lies partly in what they demonstrate mechanistically: plant nuclei are repeatedly exposed to and can retain exogenous sequence over deep time. Some viral sequences may later be co-opted, but the evidence base for broad adaptive domestication remains much thinner than the evidence for integration itself. [[36]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10377300/)[[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12451028/)

### 5) Broad plant prevalence claims versus recent reanalysis

The current plant literature contains a real tension that should be kept explicit rather than harmonized away. More permissive reviews in 2025 state that sequencing across the green lineage has revealed **hundreds of HGTs** and sometimes frame HGT as a major force in plant evolution. [[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)[[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12451028/) But a 2025 reassessment of published interkingdom plant candidates reanalyzed **1,170 genes** and concluded that only **343 cases**, or **29.3%**, still retained phylogenetic signal consistent with interkingdom HGT after updated homology searches and manual reevaluation. [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)

The 2026 “last-one-out” perspective sharpened this critique by arguing that many anomalous plant trees are more parsimoniously explained by **ancestral presence plus differential loss** than by repeated interkingdom transfer. It also reported that **188** previously claimed cases lost support once newly identified homologs were added. [[29]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1789570/full)[[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/) The analytical consequence is significant: plant HGT is not disappearing, but the field is moving from “hundreds of candidates” toward a smaller subset of **robust, mechanism-aware, phylogenetically resilient cases**.

A final asymmetry is worth stating plainly. The supplied evidence strongly supports **bacteria-to-plant**, **plant-to-plant**, **fungus-to-plant**, and **virus-to-plant** categories, but it does **not** provide equally strong, named **plant-to-bacteria** case studies. That evidentiary asymmetry itself is informative: the current consensus on non-microbial eukaryotic HGT is driven by transfers *into* plant genomes, not by a balanced reciprocal literature in both directions.

## Documented HGT in animals

Animal HGT is real, but compared with plants it is narrower in distribution and more deeply shaped by past false positives. The strongest modern case set is concentrated in **invertebrates**, especially taxa with **intracellular symbionts**, **plant-feeding or parasitic lifestyles**, or unusual genomic/ecological features such as **bdelloid rotifers**. By contrast, the most sensational broad claims in vertebrates, humans, and some micrometazoans have often failed reanalysis. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)
