### 1) Endosymbiont-to-animal transfer: common as insertion, uncommon as proven adaptation

The most repeatedly documented route into animal genomes is transfer from intracellular symbionts, especially **Wolbachia**. This donor is unusually favorable for heritable transfer because it is **maternally inherited**, intracellular, and often present in tissues linked to reproduction. Reviews estimate Wolbachia infects roughly **20–70% of insect species**, and a 2024 insect-focused paper summarized the frequency as **over 50%**. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[59]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/insect-science/articles/10.3389/finsc.2024.1519986/full)

This ecology matters mechanistically. Wolbachia does not need to cross external barriers to reach the host’s germline-associated environment; it is already there. That immediately makes animal HGT from endosymbionts more plausible than one-off transfer from an external free-living bacterium. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[59]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/insect-science/articles/10.3389/finsc.2024.1519986/full)

Large Wolbachia-derived inserts are secure examples of sequence movement. In **Drosophila ananassae**, an entire approximately **1.4 Mb** Wolbachia genome was reported integrated into a host chromosome. In the bean beetle **Callosobruchus chinensis**, roughly **30%** of a Wolbachia genome was found on the X chromosome. A 2007 survey summarized in later reviews found evidence of HGT in **8 of 11** Wolbachia-containing arthropod and nematode genomes then available, leading one review to suggest that perhaps **~70%** of infected hosts may carry a nuclear insert—while also cautioning that the sample was small and non-random. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)

These cases are important, but they also illustrate a key conceptual divide: **insertion is not the same as adaptation**. Many Wolbachia-derived fragments appear to be genomic fossils or low-level transcripts rather than domesticated host genes. The *D. ananassae* case is therefore crucial not because it proves adaptive innovation, but because it proves that very large donor-to-animal nuclear transfers can occur. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2647948/)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)

The strongest functional exception is the **f element** in the pillbug **Armadillidium vulgare**, a large Wolbachia-derived insertion implicated in **female sex determination**. This is one of the clearest known cases where symbiont-to-host transfer has a specific evolutionary role rather than merely leaving a genomic scar. [[91]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28753988/)

A smaller but plausible domestication case is in **Aedes aegypti**, where two adjacent genes show bacterial-like structure, synteny to Wolbachia genes, expression in both sexes, and persistence in another *Aedes* species, consistent with old integration and likely function, though direct phenotype evidence remains more limited than in the best metabolic cases. [[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2647948/)

### 2) Fungal-to-animal transfer: carotenoid biosynthesis in aphids and mites

The most canonical adaptive animal HGT case remains **carotenoid biosynthesis in aphids**. The 2010 *Science* paper showed that aphids carry multiple carotenoid biosynthesis genes of **fungal origin**, integrated into the aphid genome and expanded by duplication. A specific desaturase difference correlates with red versus green aphid coloration, and these color morphs have ecological consequences because predators and parasitoids differentially attack them. [[64]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/)[[65]](https://www.nationalgeographic.com/science/article/aphids-got-their-colours-by-stealing-genes-from-fungi)[[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)

This case remains unusually strong because it combines nearly every evidence type the field values: clear donor affinity, physical presence in the aphid genome, gene-family evolution after transfer, and a visible organismal phenotype with ecological relevance. It is one of the few animal HGT examples that genuinely clears both the **authenticity** and **adaptive function** thresholds. [[64]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/)[[65]](https://www.nationalgeographic.com/science/article/aphids-got-their-colours-by-stealing-genes-from-fungi)[[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)

A related case occurs in **spider mites**, where fungal-origin carotenoid biosynthesis genes are expressed in green and red forms and vary with pigmentation and diapause. Spider mites also possess a horizontally acquired **cyanase** with experimentally demonstrated enzymatic activity and expression responses linked to host-plant use. [[54]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21920958/)[[53]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22960016/) Together, aphids and mites show that HGT into animals is not limited to bacterial donors or symbiont fragments; eukaryote-to-animal transfer can also supply useful metabolic capacity.

### 3) HGT and herbivory/parasitism: nematodes and other plant-associated animals

Many of the strongest animal HGTs are tied directly to **feeding ecology**. Plant tissues contain structural polymers that animals usually cannot digest unaided, so genes encoding **plant cell-wall-degrading enzymes (PCWDEs)** are exactly the sort of discrete ecological tool that HGT can supply. In **plant-parasitic nematodes**, the accepted HGT repertoire includes cellulases, pectate lyases, xylanases, and polygalacturonases. These genes are typically absent from animals, group phylogenetically with microbial donors, are expressed in parasitic gland cells, and are tightly linked to root penetration and host exploitation. [[51]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/)[[70]](https://backoffice.biblio.ugent.be/download/1971253/6763640)

The causal logic here is strong: these enzymes give animals a biochemical capability they largely lack, and that capability directly mediates parasitic success. In some nematodes, knockdown reduces infectivity, and in **Pristionchus** cellulases the evidence extends to enzyme activity, persistence of the gene family over time, turnover, and signatures of selection. A GH12 cellulase in **Xiphinema index** also shows biochemical activity consistent with bacterial-origin acquisition. [[69]](https://etheses.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/34944/1/Megan%20Clarke%20Thesis%20April%202024.pdf)[[120]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3032686/)[[121]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5664137/)

Reviews extend this general picture to herbivorous arthropods as well, where transferred genes contribute to plant-cell-wall penetration, nutrient assimilation, or detoxification. The exact donor histories are better resolved in nematodes and whiteflies than across all arthropod cases, but the broader pattern is consistent: animal HGT is most convincing when it explains a very specific ecological innovation. [[49]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4943190/)[[71]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0965174814001088)

### 4) Plant-to-animal HGT: whiteflies as a benchmark case

One of the most striking recent cases is **plant-to-whitefly HGT**. In **Bemisia tabaci**, the plant-derived gene **BtPMaT1** encodes a phenolic glucoside malonyltransferase that allows the insect to **detoxify plant defense compounds**. This is unusually strong not just because the gene is plant-like, but because function was tested directly: **silencing BtPMaT1 through plant-mediated RNA interference rendered tomato effectively resistant to whiteflies**. [[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)

This case is important for three reasons. First, it is a clear example of **plant-to-animal** transfer rather than microbial-to-animal transfer. Second, it shows an immediately intelligible adaptive mechanism: the whitefly becomes better able to tolerate plant chemical defense. Third, it offers rare causal leverage from functional genetics rather than inference alone. [[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)

A later study identified **49 plant-like genes** in whitefly genomes, inferred to reflect at least **24 independent HGT events**. Orthologs across multiple cryptic whitefly species, expression, and purifying selection strongly argue against contamination. [[117]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9599486/)[[118]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36155788/) Even if not every gene in that set proves equally important, the whitefly lineage now stands as one of the clearest animal examples in which HGT has repeatedly contributed to host-plant interaction.

### 5) Bdelloid rotifers: the clearest animal hotspot, but still not “microbial-like”

**Bdelloid rotifers** are the best-supported animal lineage with unusually high foreign-gene content. Multiple studies report roughly **8–10%** of genes or transcripts as foreign in origin, and a 2024 study found a background frequency of about **11.5%** high-confidence HGT candidates in two species. [[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)[[103]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11258130/)[[104]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168952524000283)

This makes bdelloids an outlier among animals, but not a microbial analogue. The current interpretation is **slow accumulation over long evolutionary time**, not wholesale genomic promiscuity. One study estimated roughly **12.8 gains** and **2.0 losses** of foreign genes per million years, while a later synthesis described the net pace as on the order of one foreign gene per **100,000 years**. [[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)[[103]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11258130/)

Bdelloids are also notable because they provide stronger functional evidence than many other animal hotspots. When challenged with a fungal pathogen, HGT-derived genes were reported to be **2–3 times enriched** among upregulated genes, and some bacterial-like biosynthetic clusters were induced much more strongly in a resistant species than in a susceptible one. [[115]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-49919-1)[[103]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11258130/) That does not mean every foreign bdelloid gene is adaptive, but it does show that at least some have been integrated into defense-related biology.

### 6) Strongly contested or refuted animal claims

The animal HGT field is partly defined by failures that improved later standards:

- **Human genome HGT claims** from 2001 and later reassertions were rejected after broader phylogenetic sampling and reanalysis. Aside from organelle-derived DNA and retrovirus-related events, there is no credible evidence for widespread bacterial or fungal gene acquisition in humans. [[13]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11418856/)[[17]](https://www.jcvi.org/media-center/more-extensive-analysis-casts-doubt-claim-bacterial-genes-humans)[[15]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13059-017-1214-2)[[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)
- The **tardigrade** claim that approximately **17.5%** of genes were foreign fell to **at most 1–2%** after contamination-aware reassembly, with only about **0.4%** strong non-metazoan candidates total. [[21]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1510461112)[[24]](https://www.pacb.com/publications/no-evidence-for-extensive-horizontal-gene-transfer-in-the-genome-of-the-tardigrade-hypsibius-dujardini/)[[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)
- In the kleptoplastic sea slug **Elysia chlorotica**, analysis of **egg DNA** found no evidence that algal genes had entered the slug germ line, weakening a once-popular HGT-based explanation for long-term plastid retention. [[58]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3708498/)

These are not peripheral embarrassments; they are central to current consensus because they show exactly how contamination, missing homologs, or wishful interpretation can mimic HGT in animals.

## Mechanisms enabling HGT in eukaryotes

The mechanistic literature supports a narrower conclusion than some broad evolutionary discussions imply: **multiple routes are plausible, but only a few are directly demonstrated or repeatedly supported in non-microbial eukaryotes**. The most robustly supported routes are not the same in plants and animals.

### Comparison of proposed mechanisms

| Mechanism | Plants vs animals | Best evidence | What it explains well | Main limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Agrobacterium-like DNA transfer** | Primarily plants | Natural T-DNA transfer into plant genomes; sweet potato, *Nicotiana*, *Linaria*. [[80]](https://journals.plos.org/plospathogens/article)[[81]](https://www.annualreviews.org/doi/10.1146/annurev-phyto-082718-100101)[[82]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26968003/)[[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/)[[123]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25902487/) | Direct interkingdom movement, nuclear integration, heritable transfer in plants | Strongly demonstrated in plants, not a general animal route |
| **Grafting / parasitic interfaces** | Mainly plants | Whole chloroplast genome transfer across grafts in *Nicotiana*; repeated host-parasite transfers in haustorial systems. [[84]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3289295/)[[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6023873/)[[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/) | Why plant-to-plant transfer is enriched in grafted or parasitic contexts | Does not by itself prove nuclear domestication in every case |
| **Endosymbiont DNA leakage** | Mainly animals | Repeated Wolbachia inserts into arthropod and nematode genomes. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[89]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3798261/)[[91]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28753988/) | Why animal HGT clusters around intracellular symbionts | Often yields inserts with little demonstrated host function |
| **Viral mediation / endogenization** | Both, especially plants | Widespread EVEs and endogenous caulimovirids. [[92]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28672159/)[[36]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10377300/)[[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12451028/) | Frequent movement of sequence into nuclei over deep time | Hard to prove any given adaptive host gene came via a virus |
| **Transposable-element movement** | Both | Common for mobile DNA, sometimes between species. [[79]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5791352/)[[92]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28672159/) | Movement of DNA modules and integration potential | Often easier to document TE transfer than conventional gene domestication |
| **DNA repair-mediated capture at breaks** | Both | Implicated in some integrations, including the pillbug **f element**. [[91]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28753988/)[[97]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32611311/) | Conversion of donor fragments into permanent chromosomal inserts | Usually inferred after the fact rather than directly observed |
| **Weak-link stages / desiccation-associated uptake** | Suggested for some animals and plants | Discussed for bdelloids and exposed eggs/early stages. [[14]](https://carollee.labs.wisc.edu/Evolution410_Reading/Soucy2015Building%20web%20of%20life.pdf)[[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)[[97]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32611311/) | Why a few lineages may be unusually permissive | Plausible, but not a demonstrated general mechanism |

### Mechanistic detail that matters causally

In plants, **Agrobacterium** remains the clearest demonstration that natural interkingdom transfer into a multicellular eukaryote is mechanistically possible. Bacterial **vir** functions process donor DNA, move it into wounded plant cells, traffic it toward the nucleus, and facilitate integration. Wounding matters because phenolic compounds released by injured tissue activate the transfer system. [[80]](https://journals.plos.org/plospathogens/article)[[81]](https://www.annualreviews.org/doi/10.1146/annurev-phyto-082718-100101)[[82]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26968003/)[[83]](https://microbenotes.com/agrobacterium-mediated-gene-transfer/) The evolutionary significance is practical: once a route exists that can move DNA into cells that contribute to reproduction, stable HGT no longer requires speculation.

Plants also possess **physical continuity interfaces** that animals generally lack. Whole chloroplast genome transfer has been experimentally reconstructed across **graft junctions** between sexually incompatible *Nicotiana* species, and parasitic haustoria provide long-lived conduits for exchange of transcripts, proteins, and nucleic acids between host and parasite. In **Cuscuta**, evidence indicates transfer of **genomic DNA**, not merely RNA. [[84]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3289295/)[[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6023873/)[[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/) This helps explain why plants, especially parasitic plants and grasses with close tissue contact, often show broader recurring HGT than animals.

In animals, the clearest repeated route is **intracellular symbiont leakage**, especially from **Wolbachia**. The donor is already inside host cells and often near germline-associated tissues, so the hardest step—access to a heritable lineage—is partly pre-solved. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[89]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3798261/)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf) This is why Wolbachia dominates the literature on authentic animal inserts even though most inserts do not become adaptive host genes.

The **weak-link model** offers a useful cross-lineage explanation for why HGT can still occur despite these barriers: early embryos, spores, exposed eggs, or other stages may be unusually permissive to foreign DNA. In unicellular eukaryotes every cell is both soma and germ line, whereas in multicellular plants and animals only certain stages can transmit an acquisition to descendants. [[14]](https://carollee.labs.wisc.edu/Evolution410_Reading/Soucy2015Building%20web%20of%20life.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[77]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4033532/) The model is suggestive, but it is more successful as a framework for where to look than as a universally demonstrated mechanism.

## Why HGT is rare in plants and animals

HGT is rare in plants and animals not because a single hard barrier makes it impossible, but because successful cases must clear a **stack of independent filters**. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[77]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4033532/)

### 1) Germline and heritability barriers

In animals, heritable HGT usually requires access to the **germ line** or to an early developmental stage that contributes to it. Germ cells are physically sheltered and molecularly defended, so most somatic acquisitions are evolutionary dead ends. This is one reason animal HGT is especially sparse in vertebrates and more evident in invertebrates with intimate symbiont associations or exposed early stages. [[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[77]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4033532/)[[79]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5791352/)[[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)

Plants do not have an animal-style permanently segregated germ line, which may make them somewhat more permissive. But this is only a relative advantage, not a free pass: the foreign sequence still must enter cells whose descendants contribute to reproduction, survive developmental screening, and persist through meiosis and population-level selection. [[2]](http://www.ask-force.org/web/HorizontalGT/Keeling-HGT-Eukaryotic-Evolution-2008.pdf)[[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)[[105]](https://www.nature.com/articles/ncomms2148)

### 2) Cellular and chromosomal barriers

Even before selection acts, foreign DNA faces physical challenges: cell walls in plants, plasma-membrane entry, nuclear-envelope access, chromatin incorporation, and stable integration into a chromosome. Eukaryotic genomes are compartmentalized and heavily regulated, making successful integration far less straightforward than in many microbial contexts. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[88]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2022.971402/full)[[77]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4033532/)

### 3) Regulatory incompatibility

A transferred gene that lacks usable **promoters, enhancers, intron patterns, or splicing signals** may remain silent or misexpressed. Plant reviews note that older successful bacterial-origin genes often acquired introns after transfer, implying substantial remodeling was necessary before stable host use. [[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)[[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/) This is a major causal reason why the successful eukaryotic HGTs tend to be genes with relatively modular functions rather than components of deeply integrated core pathways.

### 4) Network-integration and dosage costs

Keeling’s 2024 synthesis argues that eukaryotic HGT cannot be understood by exposure alone; **selection** is decisive. A transferred gene must fit into preexisting cellular networks, and many foreign genes probably impose dosage, folding, localization, or interaction costs rather than benefits. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf) This predicts exactly the empirical pattern observed: successful transfers usually involve **narrow ecological tools**—detoxification enzymes, cell-wall-degrading enzymes, pathogen-defense proteins, pigment pathways—rather than wholesale rewiring of core transcription, translation, or development. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)

### 5) Genome defense and purifying selection

Most incoming DNA is expected to be silenced, degraded, or lost by **purifying selection**. Reviews on mobile DNA emphasize that animal germ lines deploy defenses such as **piRNA-mediated transposon silencing**, and analogous genome-defense processes in eukaryotes reduce the long-term survival of foreign inserts. [[79]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5791352/)[[99]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3504300/)[[100]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168952522000361)

### 6) Fixation barriers in sexual populations

Even a genuine, integrated transfer in one individual must spread through a population to become an evolutionary event. In large sexual populations, especially when the effect is neutral or slightly deleterious, most inserts are expected to disappear. This helps explain why so many accepted eukaryotic HGTs are **old survivors** rather than signs of constant, rampant turnover. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)

## How common HGT appears to be in eukaryotic species

### Comparative prevalence estimates

| Lineage / scope | Estimate | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Prokaryotes** | Some syntheses estimate roughly **75–80%** of genes have experienced HGT at some point. [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/veterinary-science-and-veterinary-medicine/horizontal-gene-transfer)[[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4962295/) | HGT is a routine, central evolutionary force. |
| **Most eukaryotic nuclear genomes** | Usually **at most a few percent** detectably derived from HGT. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf) | Much rarer than in microbes and strongly method-dependent. |
| **Obligate parasitic plants** | About **0.1–0.2%** of genes estimated as host-derived in one comparative analysis. [[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/) | Low genomic fraction, but concentrated in lineages with intimate host contact. |
| **Plant interkingdom candidate literature** | **1,170** published candidates reanalyzed; only **343** (**29.3%**) remained HGT-consistent. [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/) | Published plant totals were likely substantially inflated. |
| **Bdelloid rotifers** | Roughly **8–10%** foreign genes; about **11.5%** high-confidence background in a 2024 study. [[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)[[103]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11258130/)[[104]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168952524000283) | Exceptional animal hotspot, but still far below many microbial estimates and accumulated over long time. |
| **Tardigrade after reanalysis** | At most **1–2%** foreign genes; strong candidates about **0.4%** total. [[24]](https://www.pacb.com/publications/no-evidence-for-extensive-horizontal-gene-transfer-in-the-genome-of-the-tardigrade-hypsibius-dujardini/) | Original dramatic estimate was artefactual. |
| **Wolbachia-infected host genomes (small early sample)** | **8 of 11** examined genomes showed inserts; one review suggested perhaps **~70%** of infected hosts may carry a nuclear insert, based on a small, non-random sample. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/) | Insertions may be common in this ecological context, but most are not clearly adaptive. |
| **Insects broadly (secondary synthesis)** | Over **1,410 genes** across **218 genomes** via **741 events**. [[72]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1226861524000219) | Suggests repeated HGT in insects, but evidence quality is heterogeneous across cases. |

### What “commonness” actually means

The prevalence question is frequently muddled because three different quantities are being mixed:

1. **Entry frequency:** how often foreign DNA reaches a plant or animal genome. This may be relatively frequent for viruses, symbiont fragments, or transposable elements. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[36]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10377300/)[[79]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5791352/)  
2. **Resident-sequence frequency:** how often foreign DNA becomes a stable, vertically inherited genomic resident. This is much rarer. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)  
3. **Adaptive domestication frequency:** how often a foreign sequence acquires demonstrable host benefit. This is rarer still, but clearly nonzero. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)[[64]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/)[[115]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-49919-1)

This distinction explains an apparent paradox. Some lineages may experience many DNA-entry events, especially via viruses or endosymbionts, without accumulating many functional HGT-derived host genes. In other words, **sequence movement is more common than successful gene domestication**. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[36]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10377300/)[[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2647948/)

### Plants versus animals on prevalence

The best current comparison is not that plants have “a lot” and animals have “a little,” but that the **distribution of convincing cases differs**.

- **Plants** show broader recurring evidence across multiple contexts: parasitic plant mitochondria, grass-to-grass transfer, Agrobacterium-mediated insertion, and viral endogenization. [[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)[[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/)[[36]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10377300/)
- **Animals** show fewer recurring contexts, but some very strong hotspots: Wolbachia-associated insertions, whitefly plant-derived genes, plant-parasitic nematode enzymes, and bdelloid accumulation. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)[[51]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/)[[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)

So the most defensible statement is: **plants appear to show broader lineage-level recurrence, whereas animals show narrower but sometimes deeper ecological hotspots**.

## Functional consequences and whether transferred genes confer benefits

The evidence does support beneficial HGT in non-microbial eukaryotes—but selectively, not routinely. The field is most persuasive when adaptive claims are restricted to cases with **mechanistic phenotype evidence**, not merely sequence similarity or transcription. [[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)

### Beneficial transfers versus neutral or dubious cases

| Category | Representative cases | Why they are convincing or not |
|---|---|---|
| **Strongly supported beneficial transfers** | Aphid carotenoid genes; whitefly **BtPMaT1**; plant-parasitic nematode PCWDEs; **Pristionchus/Xiphinema** cellulases; bdelloid defense-related foreign genes; some **Alloteropsis** transfers; **Fhb7** if upheld. [[64]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/)[[65]](https://www.nationalgeographic.com/science/article/aphids-got-their-colours-by-stealing-genes-from-fungi)[[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)[[51]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/)[[120]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3032686/)[[121]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5664137/)[[115]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-49919-1)[[41]](https://etheses.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/31607/1/SGSH_Thesis_Corrections_upload.pdf)[[42]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32273397/)[[43]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/context/usdaarsfacpub/article/3346/viewcontent/Wang_SCIENCE_2020_Horizontal_gene_USDA.pdf) | These cases link donor-origin genes to a specific biochemical or ecological function, often with enzyme assays, knockdown, disease assays, or selection evidence. |
| **Authentic transfers with limited proven benefit** | Many Wolbachia inserts; many sweet potato or other natural T-DNAs; some grass and parasitic-plant transfers. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2647948/)[[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/)[[123]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25902487/)[[41]](https://etheses.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/31607/1/SGSH_Thesis_Corrections_upload.pdf)[[39]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20508124/) | Authenticity is often secure, but phenotype evidence is partial or absent. |
| **Mostly neutral, fossil, or unclear foreign DNA** | Many endogenous viral elements in plants; large decaying symbiont inserts. [[36]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10377300/)[[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12451028/)[[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/) | Strong evidence of integration, weak evidence of host advantage. |
| **Dubious or artifactual claims** | Human bacterial-gene claims; tardigrade **17.5%** estimate; some earlier broad plant interkingdom lists. [[13]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11418856/)[[15]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13059-017-1214-2)[[24]](https://www.pacb.com/publications/no-evidence-for-extensive-horizontal-gene-transfer-in-the-genome-of-the-tardigrade-hypsibius-dujardini/)[[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[29]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1789570/full) | Collapse after contamination control, better sampling, or alternative-history testing. |

### Where functional evidence is strongest

The best adaptive cases share a common logic: they import a capability that the recipient lineage otherwise lacks or possesses only weakly.

- **Pigment and defense chemistry:** aphid carotenoid genes alter pigmentation with ecological consequences. [[64]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/)[[65]](https://www.nationalgeographic.com/science/article/aphids-got-their-colours-by-stealing-genes-from-fungi)
- **Detoxification of host defenses:** whitefly **BtPMaT1** enables detoxification of phenolic glucosides; silencing it sharply reduces pest success. [[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)
- **Mechanical access to food/hosts:** nematode PCWDEs enable penetration and digestion of plant tissues. [[51]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/)[[69]](https://etheses.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/34944/1/Megan%20Clarke%20Thesis%20April%202024.pdf)[[70]](https://backoffice.biblio.ugent.be/download/1971253/6763640)
- **Pathogen resistance:** **Fhb7** is claimed to detoxify trichothecenes and confer Fusarium resistance. [[42]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32273397/)[[43]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/context/usdaarsfacpub/article/3346/viewcontent/Wang_SCIENCE_2020_Horizontal_gene_USDA.pdf)[[44]](https://files01.core.ac.uk/download/334984681.pdf)
- **Defense systems in unusual lineages:** bdelloid HGT-derived genes are enriched among induced transcripts during pathogen challenge. [[115]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-49919-1)[[103]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11258130/)

By contrast, the weakest adaptive claims tend to involve cases where the main evidence is simply that a foreign sequence is present and perhaps transcribed. That is why most **Wolbachia** insertions remain functionally ambiguous despite secure integration. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2647948/)

## Evolutionary significance, debates, and open questions

## What the current evidence supports

The best synthesis is a constrained but important one:

- HGT is **not** a negligible curiosity in non-microbial eukaryotes. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)
- HGT is **not** a microbial-style dominant force shaping most plant and animal nuclear genomes. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4962295/)
- HGT is best viewed as a **rare but sometimes decisive source of innovation**, especially during ecological transitions involving herbivory, parasitism, defense, host shifts, or specialized metabolism. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)

This interpretation is stronger than a mere compromise because it explains the empirical distribution of accepted cases. The strongest examples are exactly those where a transferred gene can act as a modular ecological solution—pigment synthesis, detoxification, cell-wall degradation, pathogen resistance—rather than requiring deep rewiring of core eukaryotic information systems. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)[[64]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/)[[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)[[51]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/)

## Plants versus animals: evolutionary pattern

**Plants** currently appear to show **more recurrent and mechanistically varied HGT** than animals. The main reasons are not mysterious: plants have repeated access points through **wounds**, **grafts**, **haustorial host-parasite interfaces**, **Agrobacterium-like transfer systems**, and perhaps less permanently segregated reproductive development than animals. [[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)[[84]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3289295/)[[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6023873/)[[80]](https://journals.plos.org/plospathogens/article)[[2]](http://www.ask-force.org/web/HorizontalGT/Keeling-HGT-Eukaryotic-Evolution-2008.pdf)

**Animals**, by contrast, show a narrower recurrent donor set and stronger heritability constraints. Their most convincing HGTs cluster where those barriers are locally relaxed or bypassed—**intracellular symbionts** like Wolbachia, **plant-feeding or parasitic lifestyles** where foreign metabolic genes are directly useful, and unusual lineages such as **bdelloid rotifers**. [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)[[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)

Yet the single strongest known non-microbial-eukaryote hotspot in the evidence surveyed here is actually animal, not plant: **bdelloid rotifers**. So the cleanest high-level comparison is not “plants more, animals less” in absolute terms, but **plants broader across contexts, animals spikier across hotspots**. [[103]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11258130/)[[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)[[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)

## Strongly supported versus contested claims

One of the clearest signs that the field is maturing is that improved data has done two opposite things at once:

- It has **validated** some striking cases beyond reasonable doubt: sweet potato T-DNAs, aphid carotenoid genes, whitefly plant-derived genes, nematode cellulases, many bdelloid foreign genes, and grass-to-grass transfers in **Alloteropsis**. [[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/)[[123]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25902487/)[[64]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/)[[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)[[117]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9599486/)[[120]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3032686/)[[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)[[41]](https://etheses.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/31607/1/SGSH_Thesis_Corrections_upload.pdf)
- It has **collapsed** weak or overinterpreted cases: human bacterial-gene claims, the tardigrade 17.5% estimate, some bdelloid inter-individual exchange claims, and many broad plant interkingdom candidate lists. [[15]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13059-017-1214-2)[[24]](https://www.pacb.com/publications/no-evidence-for-extensive-horizontal-gene-transfer-in-the-genome-of-the-tardigrade-hypsibius-dujardini/)[[113]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30017483/)[[114]](https://retractionwatch.com/2018/07/24/the-data-and-findings-are-unreliable-authors-explain-how-a-refutation-came-to-be-published-in-the-same-journal-as-the-original/)[[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[29]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1789570/full)

That is not a contradiction. It shows that the relevant question has shifted from **“Does HGT exist in plants and animals?”** to **“Which specific cases survive stringent contamination-aware, taxon-rich, function-aware analysis?”** [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[111]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10898089/)

## Open questions up to the present evidence base

1. **Mechanistic routes beyond the best-demonstrated systems:** outside Agrobacterium-like transfer, graft/haustorial exchange, and symbiont leakage, many proposed routes remain plausible but only indirectly supported. [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)[[92]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28672159/)  
2. **True event rates:** outside bdelloids and a few well-studied systems, lineage-by-lineage rates remain poorly constrained. [[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)[[103]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11258130/)  
3. **Retention bias:** why do some lineages, especially bdelloids, retain unusually many foreign genes? Desiccation and weak-link exposure are plausible contributors, but not universal explanations. [[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)[[97]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32611311/)[[104]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168952524000283)  
4. **Taxon-sampling bias:** how many current HGT candidates will disappear as more eukaryotic homologs are sequenced, and how many ancient transfers are currently invisible because donors or sister lineages remain unsampled? [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[29]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1789570/full)  
5. **Function versus residency:** what fraction of authentic foreign inserts are actually adaptive rather than neutral passengers or slowly decaying genomic fossils? [[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[36]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10377300/)  
6. **Improved standards:** long-read assemblies, pangenomes, contamination screening, and better population sampling should make future claims both fewer and stronger. [[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)[[111]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10898089/)[[29]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1789570/full)

## Conclusion

The current consensus is that **horizontal gene transfer in non-microbial eukaryotes is real, rare in most lineages, and evolutionarily important in a minority of cases**. Plants and animals are **not microbial-like HGT systems**: their nuclear genomes are still shaped primarily by vertical inheritance, duplication, loss, and other standard eukaryotic processes. But the older view that HGT is essentially irrelevant outside microbes is no longer defensible. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)

The strongest evidence is concentrated in specific contexts: **Agrobacterium-mediated natural transgenesis**, **plant-to-plant transfer in parasitic plants and grasses**, **fungal or bacterial metabolic genes domesticated in animals**, **Wolbachia-derived insertions into animal genomes**, **bdelloid foreign-gene accumulation**, and **widespread viral endogenization in plants**. [[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/)[[123]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25902487/)[[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/)[[41]](https://etheses.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/31607/1/SGSH_Thesis_Corrections_upload.pdf)[[64]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/)[[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)[[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)[[36]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10377300/)

The reason HGT remains rare is mechanistic and selective rather than mysterious. A successful transfer must clear multiple barriers at once: **entry**, **integration**, **access to reproductive lineages**, **regulatory compatibility**, **escape from silencing and purifying selection**, and **population-level fixation**. Those filters are far weaker in microbial systems than in plants and especially animals. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[77]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4033532/)[[79]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5791352/)[[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12448838/)

On prevalence, the most defensible summary remains that **most eukaryotic nuclear genomes contain at most a few percent detectably HGT-derived genes**, often much less, while a few lineages such as **bdelloid rotifers** are clear outliers. In plants, recent reanalysis shows that many published interkingdom candidate lists were overcalled: of **1,170** published candidates reexamined, only **343** (**29.3%**) remained HGT-consistent. [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/)[[103]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11258130/)[[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)

On function, the asymmetry is crucial. **Documented beneficial transfers do exist**—aphid carotenoid genes, whitefly **BtPMaT1**, nematode cell-wall-degrading enzymes, some bdelloid defense genes, and possibly **Fhb7**—but **many authentic foreign sequences are neutral, fossilized, or functionally unresolved**, especially large Wolbachia inserts and many endogenous viral elements. [[64]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/)[[116]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641)[[51]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/)[[115]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-49919-1)[[42]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32273397/)[[43]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/context/usdaarsfacpub/article/3346/viewcontent/Wang_SCIENCE_2020_Horizontal_gene_USDA.pdf)[[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/)[[36]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10377300/)

The most honest high-level conclusion is therefore this: **HGT in plants and animals is rare enough that every accepted case requires exceptional evidence, but real enough that it changes evolutionary interpretation when it occurs.** It is best understood not as a universal engine of eukaryotic genome evolution, but as a **sporadic, strongly filtered, sometimes transformative source of novelty whose apparent prevalence is still being recalibrated by cleaner assemblies, denser taxon sampling, stricter phylogenetic tests, and sharper functional assays.** [[1]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf)[[6]](http://mccutcheonlab.org/s/17_husnik_nrm.pdf)[[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)[[29]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1789570/full)
